# The Peer-Programme Survey {#sec-peer-survey}

Several chapters of this book justify their inclusion by
reference to a survey of the graduate curricula of peer United
States biostatistics programmes: @sec-shell on the command
line, @sec-deident on de-identification, @sec-databases on SQL,
@sec-missing on missing data, @sec-targets on pipeline tools,
@sec-ci on continuous integration, and @sec-sas on SAS. This
appendix records the sources on which those claims rest, so
that a reader can check them.

## Scope

The survey covers master's-level curricula in biostatistics and
allied statistical-computing courses at United States
institutions. It was conducted by consulting the public course
catalogues, syllabi, and course pages listed below. Where a
programme's catalogue was the only public record, the catalogue
is cited; where an individual course published a syllabus, the
syllabus is cited in preference, because it states the
software and the topics rather than merely the title.

Two properties of the evidence base deserve statement, because
they bound what the survey can support. First, a public
catalogue records what a programme says it teaches, not what is
taught; a topic covered in a single lecture and a topic given a
whole course both appear as a line in a syllabus, and the
survey cannot distinguish them except where the syllabus is
detailed. Second, curricula change annually, and the sources
below were consulted in the years their entries record. A claim
of the form 'most peer programmes teach X' should be read as a
claim about the published curricula at that time, not as a
durable fact about the field.

## Sources consulted

Course-level sources, where a syllabus or course page was
available:

| Institution | Course | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Johns Hopkins | Statistical Computing (140.776) | [@hopkins-biostat776-2024] |
| UNC Chapel Hill | Statistical Computing (BIOS 735) | [@unc-bios735-2024] |
| U. Washington | Statistical Computing (BIOST 561) | [@uw-biost561-2024] |
| U. Washington | Statistical Methods for Missing Data (BIOST 531) | [@uw-biost531-2019] |
| U. Michigan | Statistical Computing (BIOSTAT 615) | [@umich-biostat615-2024] |
| U. Michigan | Missing Data (BIOSTAT 880) | [@umich-biostat880-2024] |
| Columbia | Advanced Statistical Computing (P8160) | [@columbia-p8160-2025] |
| UC Berkeley | Introduction to Statistical Computing (STAT 243) | [@berkeley-stat243-2024] |
| U. Florida | Statistical Programming (PHC 6089) | [@uf-phc6089-2025] |

Programme-level sources, where the public record is a catalogue
or curriculum page rather than a syllabus:

| Institution | Source |
|---|---|
| Yale | [@yale-biostat-2024] |
| Emory | [@emory-bios-2024] |
| U. Minnesota | [@umn-biostat-2024] |
| UCLA | [@ucla-biostat-2024] |
| Duke | [@duke-biostat-2024] |
| Vanderbilt | [@vanderbilt-biostat-2024] |
| U. Iowa | [@uiowa-biostat-2024] |
| U. Pittsburgh | [@pitt-biostat-2024] |
| U. Pennsylvania | [@penn-ggeb-2024] |
| Brown | [@brown-biostat-2024] |
| Boston University | [@bu-biostat-2024] |
| New York University | [@nyu-gphgu-2024] |
| Northwestern | [@northwestern-biostat-2024] |
| UT Health Houston | [@utheuston-biostat-2024] |

## Related material

The survey is a comparison of curricula, not of textbooks. Two
teaching resources outside the survey shaped this book directly
and are cited where they bear on a chapter: Jenny Bryan's STAT
545 at the University of British Columbia
[@bryan2019stat545], which is the pedagogical model for the
Practicum's approach, and Karl Broman's *Tools for Reproducible
Research* [@broman2019tools4rr], which is the closest peer
treatment of the reproducibility material in Part I. Frank
Harrell's *R Workflow for Reproducible Data Analysis and
Reporting* [@harrell2024rworkflow] covers adjacent ground from
a different angle and is the work a reader of this book should
consult next on analysis workflow specifically.
